attack_chain.description: Threat actors obtained a legitimate Amazon SES access token that had been issued to a Kaspersky third-party contractor during testing of the website 2050.earth.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: a legitimate Amazon Simple Email Service (SES) token issued to a third-party contractor was recently used by threat actors

evidence_chunks.quote: a legitimate Amazon Simple Email Service (SES) token issued to a third-party contractor was recently used by threat actors
a legitimate Amazon Simple Email Service (SES) token issued to a third-party contractor was recently used by threat actors

attack_chain.description: Attackers leveraged the trusted third-party contractor relationship (and Kaspersky's legitimate AWS/SES infrastructure) to send phishing emails that appeared to come from Kaspersky.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: they used an official Kaspersky email and sent the emails from Amazon Web Services infrastructure, which likely helped them reach their targets mailboxes by easily evading most Secure Email Gateway (SEGs) protections

evidence_chunks.quote: they used an official Kaspersky email and sent the emails from Amazon Web Services infrastructure, which likely helped them reach their targets mailboxes by easily evading most Secure Email Gateway (SEGs) protections

they used an official Kaspersky email and sent the emails from Amazon Web Services infrastructure, which likely helped them reach their targets mailboxes by easily evading most Secure Email Gateway (SEGs) protections

attack_chain.description: Attackers used the stolen SES token to send emails from noreply@sm.kaspersky.com and other Kaspersky-associated addresses, effectively compromising Kaspersky's ability to send email via SES.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: These emails have various sender addresses, including but not limited to noreply@sm.kaspersky.com.

evidence_chunks.quote: These emails have various sender addresses, including but not limited to noreply@sm.kaspersky.com.
These emails have various sender addresses, including but not limited to noreply@sm.kaspersky.com.

attack_chain.description: Attackers sent spear-phishing emails disguised as 'Fax notifications' containing links to fake Microsoft credential harvesting pages, targeting Office 365 users.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The phishing e-mails are usually arriving in the form of 'Fax notifications' and lure users to fake websites collecting credentials for Microsoft online services

evidence_chunks.quote: The phishing e-mails are usually arriving in the form of 'Fax notifications' and lure users to fake websites collecting credentials for Microsoft online services
The phishing e-mails are usually arriving in the form of 'Fax notifications' and lure users to fake websites collecting credentials for Microsoft online services

attack_chain.description: Victims who followed phishing links were directed to fake Microsoft login landing pages (using Iamtheboss and MIRCBOOT phishing kits) that harvested their Office 365 credentials.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: redirecting potential victims to phishing landing pages designed to harvest their Microsoft credentials

evidence_chunks.quote: redirecting potential victims to phishing landing pages designed to harvest their Microsoft credentials
redirecting potential victims to phishing landing pages designed to harvest their Microsoft credentials

impact_type: Phishing / Credential Harvesting (abuse of trusted email infrastructure)
initial_access_vector: A legitimate Amazon SES access token issued to a third-party contractor during testing of 2050.earth was stolen and reused by threat actors to send phishing emails from Kaspersky's AWS/SES infrastructure.
persistence_mechanism: Continued use of the valid, unrevoked SES token until Kaspersky revoked it upon discovery.
privilege_escalation: None observed; attackers operated within the privileges of the stolen SES token.
